title: Supersymmetric quantum criticality with discrete symmetry
abstract: Supersymmetry, originally proposed in high-energy physics, can emerge as a remarkable low-energy structure in condensed matter systems. While emergent supersymmetry at quantum critical points is widely discussed in models with continuous symmetries, real materials are constrained by microscopic discrete symmetries. To address this, we investigate (2+1)-dimensional Gross-Neveu-Yukawa theories coupling Dirac fermions to a complex order parameter with discrete $Z_n$ anisotropy. Using the functional renormalization group, we find that for $n>3$, the anisotropic perturbations are irrelevant at the fixed point, yielding a $\mathcal{N}=2$ Wess-Zumino supersymmetric critical point. In the ordered phase, this dangerously irrelevant anisotropy gives rise to a second characteristic length scale, $ξ'$, alongside the usual correlation length, $ξ$. By tracking mass thresholds along symmetry-broken renormalization group trajectories, we extract the exponents $ν'$ and $ν$ without imposing prior scaling assumptions. For the $Z_4$, $Z_5$, and $Z_6$ models, our results support the scaling relation $ν'/ν= 1+|y_n|/p$ with $p=2$ in the isotropic framework used here.
summary:
This work investigates supersymmetric quantum criticality in fermionic systems constrained by discrete $Z_n$ symmetries using the Functional Renormalization Group. The authors find that for $n>3$, the system flows to an $\mathcal{N}=2$ SUSY fixed point, while the ordered phase reveals a second characteristic length scale. This leads to a quantifiable scaling relation between the two critical exponents, $
u'/
u = 1 + |y_n|/2$.
main_problem: The paper investigates the emergence of supersymmetry at quantum critical points in condensed matter systems that are constrained by microscopic discrete symmetries, moving beyond models with continuous symmetries.
main_result: For $Z_n$ symmetries with $n>3$, the anisotropic perturbations are irrelevant at the critical fixed point, leading to an emergent $\mathcal{N}=2$ Wess-Zumino supersymmetric critical point. Furthermore, the ordered phase exhibits two distinct characteristic length scales, $\xi$ and $\xi'$, whose exponents satisfy a specific scaling relation.
method: The Functional Renormalization Group (FRG) method is employed, utilizing both Symmetric (SYM) and Symmetry-Broken (SSB) expansions to analyze the renormalization group flow equations.
model_or_system: The system is modeled by (2+1)-dimensional Gross-Neveu-Yukawa (GNY) theories coupling Dirac fermions to a complex bosonic order parameter exhibiting $Z_n$ anisotropy.
key_observables:
Critical exponents ($
u, 
u'$), the ratio $
u'/
u$, and the emergence of two characteristic length scales ($\xi, \xi'$).
important_parameters: The discrete symmetry index $n$ (specifically $n>3$), and the anisotropy eigenvalue $|y_n|$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Functional Renormalization Group (FRG) formalism, employing approximations like the improved local potential approximation ($	ext{LPA}'$) and assuming an isotropic Euclidean spacetime ansatz at the critical point.
figures_summary:
Figures illustrate the ordered-phase RG flows for $Z_5$ and $Z_6$ in the $(\kappa, \Lambda_{01})$ plane, and show the extraction of critical exponents ($
u, 
u', 
u'/
u$) for $Z_5$ and $Z_6$ via linear fits to the flow trajectories.
paper_structure:
The paper develops the FRG flow equations for the GNY model, first analyzing the fixed point in the symmetric phase (SYM) to confirm SUSY emergence for $n>3$. It then analyzes the flow into the ordered phase (SSB) by tracking mass thresholds to extract the two distinct critical exponents $
u$ and $
u'$, confirming the scaling relation $
u'/
u = 1 + |y_n|/2$.
why_interesting: The study provides a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding how microscopic discrete symmetries constrain and modify emergent quantum critical behavior, which is highly relevant for designing and interpreting measurements in novel quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Ordered-phase RG flow and mass flows for the Z4 model at Nf = 0.5. (a) Ordered-phase RG flow in the (κ, Λ01) plane, illustrated by three representative trajectories. (b) Flow of the dimensionful longitudinal mass ln m2 L (red) and transverse mass ln m2 T (blue) along a representative trajectory. For each mass, the dashed line corresponds to zero initial anisotropic coupling Λ01, while the solid line corresponds to nonzero initial Λ01. (c) Flow of the corresponding dimensionless masses ln m2 L (red) and ln m2 T (blue), with the same line-style convention as in (b). (d) Multi-trajectory dimensionless mass flows for different initial values of κ, used to determine the RG times tL and...

FIG. 2. Extraction of ν, ν′ and ν′/ν for the Z4 model from linear fits. (a) tL against ln |κ −κc|, whose slope gives ν. (b) tT against ln |κ −κc|, whose slope gives ν′. (c) tT against tL, whose slope gives ν′/ν. The first near-critical trajectory shown in Fig. 1(d) is excluded from the fits, and the remaining ten off-critical trajectories are used.

FIG. 3. Ordered-phase RG flows for the Z5 model at Nf = 0.5. (a) Phase portrait in the (κ, Λ01) plane. (b) Multi-trajectory dimensionless mass flows for different initial values of κ, used to determine tL and tT from the crossings ln m2 L = 0 and ln m2 T = 0.

FIG. 4. Ordered-phase RG flows for the Z6 model at Nf = 0.5. (a) Phase portrait in the (κ, Λ01) plane. (b) Multi-trajectory dimensionless mass flows for different initial values of κ, used to determine tL and tT from the crossings ln m2 L = 0 and ln m2 T = 0.
FIG. 5. Extraction of ν, ν′ and ν′/ν for the Z5 model from linear fits. (a) tL against ln |κ −κc|. (b) tT against ln |κ −κc|. (c) tT against tL.
